section.title: Hagar and Ishmael
section.sectionRationale: God has sworn to give Abram a son, but the years pass and Sarai is barren. Rather than wait, the couple take matters into their own hands through Hagar. The chapter shows the bitter fruit of trying to help God's promise along, and the surprising tenderness of God toward a used and discarded slave woman.
section.thesis: When faith grows tired of waiting, it reaches for its own solutions and reaps grief; yet the God who sees finds the runaway in the desert, hears her affliction, and shows that no one is beneath His notice.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the human scheme that produces conflict and flight; and God's pursuit of Hagar in the wilderness, where she names Him the God who sees.

units.label: The scheme and its bitter fruit

verses.text: Now Abram’s wife Sarai had borne him no children, but she had an Egyptian maidservant named Hagar.
So Sarai said to Abram, “Look now, the LORD has prevented me from bearing children. Please go to my maidservant; perhaps I can build a family by her.” And Abram listened to the voice of Sarai.
So after he had lived in Canaan for ten years, his wife Sarai took her Egyptian maidservant Hagar and gave her to Abram to be his wife.
And he slept with Hagar, and she conceived. But when Hagar realized that she was pregnant, she began to despise her mistress.

Then Sarai said to Abram, “May the wrong done to me be upon you! I delivered my servant into your arms, and ever since she saw that she was pregnant, she has treated me with contempt. May the LORD judge between you and me.”
“Here,” said Abram, “your servant is in your hands. Do whatever you want with her.” Then Sarai treated Hagar so harshly that she fled from her.

units.label: The God who finds the runaway

verses.text: Now the {{angel|angel}} of the LORD found Hagar by a spring of water in the desert, the spring along the road to Shur.
“Hagar, servant of Sarai,” he said, “where have you come from, and where are you going?” “I am running away from my mistress Sarai,” she replied.
So the angel of the LORD told her, “Return to your mistress and submit to her authority.”
Then the angel added, “I will greatly multiply your offspring so that they will be too numerous to count.”
The angel of the LORD proceeded: “Behold, you have conceived and will bear a son. And you shall name him {{ishmael|Ishmael}}, for the LORD has heard your cry of {{affliction|affliction}}.
He will be a wild donkey of a man, and his hand will be against everyone, and everyone’s hand against him; he will live in hostility toward all his brothers.”
So Hagar gave this name to the LORD who had spoken to her: “You are the {{roi|God who sees me}},” for she said, “Here I have seen the One who sees me!”
Therefore the well was called Beer-lahai-roi. It is located between Kadesh and Bered.
And Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram gave the name Ishmael to the son she had borne.
Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael to him.

angel.gloss: A messenger or angel. The angel of the LORD here speaks as God, promising to multiply Hagar's offspring, and Hagar addresses Him as the LORD Himself. Many read these appearances as the LORD condescending to be seen, a foreshadowing of the incarnation.

ishmael.gloss: The name means God hears. It is a permanent memorial that God heard the cry of a mistreated, foreign slave woman. Every time the name is spoken, it testifies that God attends to the affliction of the lowly.

affliction.gloss: Misery, affliction, hardship. The same word describes Israel's suffering in Egypt, which God also saw and heard. Hagar the Egyptian is treated as Israel would later be treated, and the God who hears Israel already hears her.

roi.gloss: God who sees me, the name Hagar coins for the LORD. She is the only person in Scripture to give God a name, and it is a name born of being noticed in her lowest moment. The well is named the well of the Living One who sees me.

basic.context: God has promised Abram a son, but Sarai is still barren after years of waiting. Following the custom of the day, she gives her slave Hagar to Abram to bear a child on her behalf. The key question is whether God's promise needs our help, or our trust, and what it costs when we stop waiting on Him.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a story that describes what happened, not a model to copy; the surrogate arrangement brings only jealousy, cruelty, and flight. The chapter's main point is the character of God, who pursues a despised runaway. Readers debate whether Sarai and Abram sinned or acted reasonably by their culture, but the fruit of the scheme speaks for itself.
basic.originalLanguages: God tells Hagar to name her son Ishmael, which means God hears, because He heard her affliction, the same word used later for Israel's misery in Egypt. In wonder she calls the LORD El-Roi, the God who sees me. Hearing and seeing are the two great mercies of this chapter.
basic.typology: The angel of the LORD who speaks as God and is called the LORD points ahead to the God who comes near and is seen, ultimately in Christ. And God's care for a rejected Gentile slave anticipates the gospel reaching beyond Israel to all the nations.
basic.christ: Jesus is the One who seeks out the despised, the outsider, and the one running away, just as the angel of the LORD sought Hagar. Paul later uses Hagar and Sarah to picture two covenants, the slavery of law-keeping and the freedom of promise fulfilled in Christ, Galatians 4.
basic.god: God is the One who sees and hears those no one else notices. He does not excuse the sin in the household, but neither does He abandon the victim of it. He meets Hagar with a promise and a future.
basic.crossReferences: Galatians 4 reads Hagar and Sarah as two covenants. Genesis 21 continues Ishmael's story. Exodus uses the same words of seeing and hearing affliction for Israel in Egypt.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows what happens when faith tries to shortcut God's promise: conflict and heartache. Yet even the detour is met by a God who sees. The true son of promise is still to come, and beyond him, Christ, in whom even outsiders like Hagar find they are seen and heard.

context.literary: The chapter is framed by Abram's age and by the barrenness that opens it and the birth that closes it. At its center is the wilderness encounter, the first appearance of the angel of the LORD in Scripture, marked by a threefold naming: Ishmael, El-Roi, and Beer-lahai-roi.
context.historical: Ancient Near Eastern custom permitted a barren wife to give her maid to her husband so the child would count as her own; the arrangement was legal and common, which is why Sarai's plan seemed reasonable in its day even as it violated the pattern of one flesh.
context.canonical: Hagar and Ishmael become the fountainhead of a great nation and, in Paul's hands, a picture of the covenant of slavery contrasted with the covenant of promise. The verbs of God seeing and hearing affliction reappear at the exodus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the grief that follows when God's people seize the promise by their own means, and to reveal the LORD as the God who sees and hears the afflicted, keeping His purposes on course despite human failure.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The surrogate scheme is narrated, not commended; its fruit is jealousy, harshness, and flight. What is prescriptive is the revealed character of God, the One who finds and provides for the lowly.

debates.question: Was Sarai and Abram's use of Hagar a sin, or a culturally acceptable act of faith gone astray?

views.label: A failure of faith
views.case: The plan echoes Eden, Sarai gives, Abram listens to the voice of his wife, and the results are strife and sorrow; the text signals a repeat of grasping rather than trusting.

views.label: A customary and understandable measure
views.case: Surrogacy through a maid was a recognized legal remedy for barrenness, and the couple may have thought they were helping the promise along in good faith.

debates.bearing: Either way the narrative shows that human shortcuts to God's promises breed conflict, while God's mercy still reaches the one who was wronged; the point is not to excuse the scheme but to marvel at grace.

study.originalLanguages: The son is to be named Yishmael, God hears, because the LORD heard Hagar's oni, her affliction, the very word Exodus uses for Israel's misery in Egypt. Then Hagar does what no one else in Scripture does: she names God, El-Roi, the God who sees me. The well becomes Beer-lahai-roi, the well of the Living One who sees me. Hearing and seeing, the two verbs, mark a God who attends to the lowest.

typology.type: The angel of the LORD who speaks and is addressed as God
typology.fulfillment: The LORD condescending to be seen and heard, anticipating the incarnation of Christ, the God who draws near.

typology.type: God's care for a rejected Egyptian slave
typology.fulfillment: The gospel going out to the nations and the outcast, gathered in Christ, Ephesians 2:12-13.

typology.type: Hagar and the covenant of slavery, Sarah and the covenant of promise
typology.fulfillment: Paul's allegory in Galatians 4, fulfilled in the freedom believers have in Christ.

study.christ: The angel of the LORD who finds the runaway and speaks with divine authority foreshadows Christ, who came to seek and to save the lost and who is God made visible. Jesus is drawn again and again to the despised, the foreigner, and the woman at the well, echoing this desert spring. Paul takes Hagar and Sarah as a picture of two covenants, and it is in Christ, the child of promise, that we are children of the free woman, born not of the flesh but of the Spirit, Galatians 4:28-31.
study.god: God is El-Roi, the God who sees, and the God who hears affliction. He does not gloss over the wrong done in Abram's household, yet He does not leave the victim of it comfortless. He meets Hagar with a question that invites her to speak, a promise for her son, and a name that will forever declare that God notices the overlooked.

crossReferences.note: Ishmael and Hagar are sent away, and again God hears the boy and provides water in the desert.
Paul reads Hagar and Sarah as two covenants, slavery and freedom, flesh and promise.
The LORD sees the affliction and hears the cry of His people, the same mercy shown to Hagar.
The eyes of the LORD are on the righteous, and His ears are open to their cry.

study.oneStory: Impatient with God's timetable, Abram and Sarai reach for the promise by their own means and reap conflict and grief, and the son they produce is not the son of promise. Yet even this detour is met by a God who sees and hears. The story presses on toward the true heir, and beyond him toward Christ, in whom the outsider, the slave, and the afflicted are all seen, heard, and welcomed.

quiz.q: Why did Sarai give Hagar to Abram?

options.text: Because she was barren and tried to secure the promised child by her own means.
options.note: Impatient with waiting, she used a customary but ultimately grievous scheme.

options.text: Because God had commanded her to do so.
options.note: It followed the custom of the day, but God never commanded it; the plan was her own.

options.text: Because Hagar asked to bear Abram's child.
options.note: Hagar was given as a servant with no say; the initiative was Sarai's.

options.text: Because Abram had rejected God's promise entirely.
options.note: Abram still held the promise; the failure was in seizing it by human means.

quiz.q: What name did Hagar give to the LORD, and why?

options.text: The God who sees me, because He noticed her in her lowest moment.
options.note: El-Roi expresses her wonder at being seen by God.

options.text: The God who provides, because He gave her water.
options.note: That naming belongs to a later scene; here the focus is on being seen.

options.text: The God of Abram, because she now served his household.
options.note: Her name for God is deeply personal, not merely borrowed from Abram.

options.text: The God who judges, because He condemned Sarai.
options.note: The name centers on God's tender attention, not on judgment.

quiz.q: What does the name Ishmael mean, and what does it testify?

options.text: God hears, testifying that the LORD heard Hagar's affliction.
options.note: The name memorializes God's attention to a mistreated slave woman.

options.text: God sees, testifying that the LORD watched over Abram.
options.note: Seeing is the theme of El-Roi; Ishmael specifically means God hears.

options.text: God laughs, testifying to Sarai's joy.
options.note: That meaning belongs to Isaac, not Ishmael.

options.text: God forgets, testifying that Hagar was abandoned.
options.note: The opposite is true; God hears and remembers her.

quiz.q: How does the angel of the LORD respond to Hagar in the desert?

options.text: He finds her, questions her gently, and gives her a promise for her son.
options.note: God pursues the runaway with care and a future.

options.text: He condemns her for fleeing and sends her away for good.
options.note: He does tell her to return, but with a promise, not condemnation.

options.text: He ignores her because she is a foreign slave.
options.note: The whole point is that God does not overlook her.

options.text: He tells her the promise is only for Sarai.
options.note: God gives Hagar her own promise of numerous offspring.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament use the figure of Hagar?

options.text: Paul reads Hagar and Sarah as two covenants, slavery and the freedom of promise.
options.note: Galatians 4 develops the allegory, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: Paul presents Hagar as the model of saving faith.
options.note: Hagar receives real mercy, but Paul's allegory casts her as the covenant of slavery.

options.text: Hagar is never mentioned again in Scripture.
options.note: She appears again in Genesis 21 and in Galatians 4.

options.text: Hagar is condemned as an enemy of God.
options.note: God shows her compassion; she is used as a picture, not condemned as a person.

questions: Sarai grew tired of waiting and took a shortcut to God's promise. Where are you tempted to force an outcome rather than trust God's timing?
The scheme brought jealousy and cruelty into the home. Have you seen how trying to control an outcome can damage the people around you?
Hagar called God 'the God who sees me.' When have you felt overlooked, and what would it mean to know that God sees you right now?
God met a foreign, mistreated slave with tenderness and a promise. Who are the overlooked people around you that God is calling you to notice?
The name Ishmael means 'God hears.' What affliction do you need to bring to God this week, trusting that He hears your cry?

translationNotes.note: Angel may also be rendered Angel with a capital, and the corresponding pronouns capitalized, since the messenger speaks and is addressed as the LORD Himself.
The Hebrew behind the God who sees me is El-Roi; the verse contains a play on seeing and being seen that is difficult to render smoothly in English.
Beer-lahai-roi means well of the Living One who sees me, tying the place forever to Hagar's confession.
